summary: I left Crane Technical High School in Chicago in March 1943 to enter the Army before completing my last year. While at Crane, I was captain of the ROTC rifle team and won the William Randolph Hearst Trophy. The school was part of my early life on the Northwest Side, near Humboldt Park, where I lived.

Chicago was my home city where I grew up after immigrating from Lithuania at age nine in 1933. I lived on the Northwest Side in the Humboldt Park area and attended Crane Technical High School there. Chicago was also where I returned after the war, using the GI Bill to complete high school and college.

While at Crane Technical High School, I was captain of the ROTC rifle team and proud to receive the William Randolph Hearst Trophy. This award recognized my marksmanship skills, which later helped me in the Army. It was a significant achievement during my high school years before I enlisted in 1943.

After returning home from Europe in Thanksgiving 1945, I used the GI Bill to go back to school. I completed my high school education in about six months and then graduated from Wright Junior College and the Industrial Engineering College of Chicago. The GI Bill was crucial in helping me further my education after the war.

I lived in the Humboldt Park area on Chicago's Northwest Side before joining the Army. It was near where I attended Crane Technical High School. Humboldt Park was part of my early American life after immigrating from Lithuania.

I was born in Lithuania and came to the United States at age nine in 1933 during the Depression. My family spoke several languages including Lithuanian, and we left just before the worst of the Nazi persecution. Sadly, my uncle Herschel and his family were victims of the Holocaust in Lithuania, with my uncle dying at Dachau after being experimented on by Dr. Mengele.

entity: European Theater of World War II
summary: I served in the European Theater of World War II for about eleven years, more than most troops. I was part of the 29th Engineer Combat Battalion, involved in combat engineering tasks like building and destroying bridges. My service took me through England, Scotland, and eventually into Nazi-occupied Europe.

My uncle Herschel was imprisoned at Dachau concentration camp during the war. He was used as a medical guinea pig by Dr. Josef Mengele until he died there. I have letters from the Dachau Museum confirming his presence, which was initially denied.

Josef Mengele was the Nazi doctor who conducted cruel medical experiments at Dachau, where my uncle Herschel was a victim. Mengele used him as a guinea pig until his death. This personal connection to Mengele's atrocities has driven me to research these events further.

I was inducted into the Army at Fort Sheridan, Illinois, in March 1943. I spent about a week there before moving on to basic training. Fort Sheridan was my first stop in the military after leaving Chicago.

After Fort Sheridan, I went to Camp Rucker, Alabama, for basic training as a combat engineer. The training was tough but I adapted well. Camp Rucker was where I learned the skills that would be essential in the European Theater.

Following basic training at Camp Rucker, we conducted maneuvers and advanced training in the fields of Tennessee. This was part of preparing us for combat engineering duties. Tennessee was a key training location before deployment.

As a combat engineer, I helped build Bailey bridges, which are steel modular bridges that can be assembled and disassembled like an erector set. These bridges were vital for crossing rivers during combat operations in Europe. We often reused them multiple times or destroyed them after use.

When I left home to join the Army, I walked onto Rockwell Street and headed for the streetcar on Division 81. It was a poignant moment promising my mother I would return. Rockwell Street marks the beginning of my military journey from Chicago.

Division Street was where I caught the streetcar after leaving home on Rockwell Street to join the Army. It was part of my daily neighborhood in Chicago’s Northwest Side. That day marked the start of my service in 1943.

After basic and advanced training in Alabama and Tennessee, we were sent to Needles, California, to the Desert Training Center at Camp Iron Mountain. The desert conditions were harsh with extreme heat, cold nights, rattlesnakes, and tarantulas. This training prepared us for the North Africa Campaign.

At the Desert Training Center near Needles, California, we trained in brutal desert conditions to prepare for combat in North Africa. The environment was challenging with dangerous wildlife and extreme temperatures. This training was crucial for our success in the North Africa Campaign.

We trained in the desert specifically for the North Africa Campaign during World War II. Although I ended up going to Europe, the desert training was intense and prepared us for harsh conditions. The campaign was a key part of the Allied effort against Nazi Germany.

We departed for Europe from Boston aboard a United States Army Transport ship. I was surprised that the ship, the USS Explorer, was taking us directly across the Atlantic to Scotland. Boston was the last American city I saw before heading overseas in late 1943 or early 1944.

I boarded a United States Army Transport ship, the USS Explorer, in Boston to cross the Atlantic to Europe. The ship was crowded with troops and part of a large convoy zig-zagging to avoid German submarines. This was my passage to the European Theater during World War II.

I came to the United States from Hamburg, Germany in 1933 as a child with my family. I remember the ship we took then was a proper ocean liner, unlike the troop transport I later took from Boston. Hamburg was the European port of departure for my immigration journey.

The USS Explorer (AP-100) was the troop transport ship that took us from Boston to Scotland in a large convoy. The voyage took about ten days, with rough weather and dangerous conditions due to enemy submarines. It was my first time crossing the Atlantic as a soldier in late 1943 or early 1944.

We arrived in Scotland after a ten-day voyage across the Atlantic aboard the USS Explorer. Specifically, we landed in Glasgow, where we were greeted by some Americans and given meat pies, which I found unappealing. Scotland was our first stop in Europe before moving on to England.

Glasgow, Scotland, was where we landed after crossing the Atlantic. We were welcomed with meat pies, which I didn’t care for. From Glasgow, we traveled by train to southern England to prepare for the invasion of Nazi-occupied Europe.

After arriving in Scotland, we took a train to southern England near the Channel, specifically to Weymouth. There we began training for the invasion of Nazi-occupied Europe. England was our base for preparation before heading into combat.

Weymouth, England, was where we were stationed after arriving from Scotland. It’s a coastal town along the Channel where we trained for the invasion of Nazi Germany. We traveled there by train and prepared for the upcoming combat operations.

Our mission was to help remove Hitler and the Nazi regime from power in Germany. I served in the European Theater fighting against Nazi Germany, building bridges, demolitions, and supporting infantry. The fight against Nazi Germany was the central purpose of my service.

Bruce Robertson was mentioned in my biographical data form, but I did not discuss him in detail during this interview. I cannot provide much about my relationship or experiences with him based on this transcript.

I served in the 29th Engineer Combat Battalion as a combat engineer during World War II. We were responsible for building and destroying bridges, supporting infantry, and other engineering tasks under fire. This unit saw extensive action in the European Theater, and I spent eleven years in Europe with them.
